sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: 20 gray buttons remain on the left panel (cols 8, 13, 18, 23). Right panel buttons all cleared. Left panel buttons on gray (g) background didn't clear when clicked in batch.
- Goal model: Need to clear remaining 20 left panel buttons, then click blue blob.
- Plan: Try clicking one left panel button individually to debug why batch didn't work, then clear all remaining.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 45 of about 72 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The click at (36, 8) changed only 1 cell at bbox [1, 17, 1, 17] - that's the timer bar at the top, not the button! The button didn't change. This is strange.
Let me check if the button is actually at a different position. Looking at the ASCII output from before:
Row 35: gggggggwggggwggggwggggwgggggg
Row 36: gggggggwggggwggggwggggwgggggg
Row 37: gggggggwggggwggggwggggwgggggg
The "w" at col 8 (0-indexed) would be at position 8 in the string. Let me count: g(0) g(1) g(2) g(3) g(4) g(5) g(6) w(7)... wait, that's col 7, not col 8.
Actually, looking at the boundary [[35, 8], [37, 8]], the column is 8. But in the ASCII string, position 8 would be the 9th character. Let me recount:
Row 35: g(0) g(1) g(2) g(3) g(4) g(5) g(6) g(7) w(8) g(9)...
Wait, that doesn't match. Let me look at the actual ASCII output again:
"Row 35: gggggggwggggwggggwggggwgggggg"
Counting: g(0) g(1) g(2) g(3) g(4) g(5) g(6) g(7) w(8) g(9) g(10) g(11) g(12) w(13) g(14) g(15) g(16) g(17) w(18) g(19) g(20) g(21) g(22) w(23) g(24) g(25) g(26) g(27) g(28) g(29)
So the "w" characters are at positions 8, 13, 18, 23. That matches the boundary columns 8, 13, 18, 23.
But when I clicked (36, 8), only the timer changed. Maybe the button needs to be clicked at a different position? Or maybe the left panel buttons work differently?
Wait, let me look at the image again. The left panel buttons are on a gray background, and they look like "I" shapes (vertical bars) and "_" shapes (horizontal bars). Maybe I need to click on the actual bar pixels, not just the center.
Actually, looking at the boundary [[35, 8], [37, 8]], this is a vertical bar spanning rows 35-37 at column 8. The center would be (36, 8). But clicking (36, 8) only changed the timer.
Hmm, maybe the issue is that the left panel is not interactive? Or maybe I need to click on the gray area above the buttons first?
Let me try clicking at a different position on the same button, like (35, 8) or (37, 8).
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the game mechanics. In level 1, the buttons were on the right panel (white background). In level 2, there are buttons on both left (gray background) and right (white background) panels. Maybe the left panel buttons are NOT the same type of buttons - maybe they're just decorative or part of a different mechanic.
Looking at the image, the left panel has:
- A large gray area (G) with a small dark square
- Below it, a lighter gray area (g) with the button-like shapes
The right panel has:
- A checkerboard area with yellow shapes
- Below it, a white area (W) with the button-like shapes
Maybe the left panel buttons are not clickable at all, and I only need to clear the right panel buttons? But then why are they there?
Actually, let me look at the bottom panels:
- Blue panel with yellow shapes
- Gray panel with yellow shape
Maybe these indicate which buttons to click? The blue panel might correspond to the right panel (blue blob is there), and the gray panel might correspond to the left panel.
Let me try a different approach. Let me check if clicking on the gray area (G) of the left panel does something.
Actually, let me first verify: are the left panel buttons really "w" color? Let me check the segmentation again.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking (36, 8) only changed the timer, not the left panel button. Left panel buttons may not be clickable or require different interaction.
Goal model: Need to figure out why left panel buttons didn't clear. Maybe they're not the same type of buttons.
Plan: Check the current state and try clicking at different positions on the left panel buttons.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state - verify left panel buttons still exist
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
w_objects = [n for n in seg if n['color'] == 'w']
print(f"Remaining gray (w) objects: {len(w_objects)}")

# Check what's at the left panel button positions in ascii
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\n=== Left panel button area ===")
for i in range(33, 50):
    print(f"Row {i}: '{ascii_lines[i][6:25]}'")

# Try clicking at the exact boundary position
# For vertical bar at col 8, rows 35-37, try row 35
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 35, 'col': 8}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
